summary: Drew DeVault provides a four-month progress report on sway, an i3-compatible Wayland compositor he created. Since the initial commit in August 2015, the project has grown to over 1,000 commits from 29 contributors. DeVault has been using sway as his daily driver and reports that 51 of 73 tracked i3 features are now supported. The post catalogs major milestones including floating windows, multihead support, IPC, and companion tools like swaybar and swaylock, while acknowledging the community contributions that made it possible.
key_points: Sway reached 1,070 commits from 29 authors in just four months, implementing 51 of 73 tracked i3 features
The project's growth has driven improvements in upstream dependencies like wlc, demonstrating a healthy open-source ecosystem effect
DeVault's role as maintainer is heavily focused on reviewing and merging community contributions rather than solo development
Remaining gaps before beta include stacking/tabbed layouts, window borders, and bar features
money_quotes: I've been using sway as my daily driver for a while now.
As the maintainer of sway, a lot of what I do is reviewing and merging contributions from others.
If you'd like to live on the edge, you can use sway right now and have a productive workflow.
This blog post was composed from sway.
key_insight: Sway's rapid progress from initial commit to daily-driver status in four months demonstrates how an i3-compatible Wayland compositor can attract a thriving contributor community by solving a clear, well-defined problem.
